skill.name: modern-javascript-patterns
skill.description: Master ES6+ features including async/await, destructuring, spread operators, arrow functions, promises, modules, iterators, generators, and functional programming patterns for writing clean, efficient JavaScript code.
skill.summary: Master ES6+ features and modern JavaScript patterns for cleaner, more maintainable code.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 89 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The scanner misinterprets JavaScript code examples in markdown documentation as security risks. Backticks in code blocks are not shell commands. References to fetch() and dynamic import() are legitimate ES6+ patterns being taught. This is a safe educational skill about modern JavaScript patterns.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection (FALSE POSITIVE)
high_findings.description: Scanner detected 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at lines 78, 80, 92, 101, 114, 118, 880. This is a FALSE POSITIVE - the scanner misinterprets JavaScript code examples (destructuring, toLowerCase(), spread operators) as cryptographic code. This is educational documentation about JavaScript syntax patterns.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: These lines contain JavaScript destructuring, string methods, and array operations - standard programming syntax, not cryptographic code.

medium_findings.title: External Command Detection (FALSE POSITIVE)
medium_findings.description: Scanner detected 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at 61 locations. This is a FALSE POSITIVE - the scanner detects backticks (`) in markdown code blocks. These are JavaScript code examples in markdown documentation, NOT shell commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Backticks in markdown denote code blocks, not shell command execution. No actual shell commands are present.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic Import Detection (FALSE POSITIVE)
medium_findings.description: Scanner detected 'Dynamic import() expression' at lines 727, 728, 732. This is a FALSE POSITIVE - dynamic import() is a legitimate ES6+ JavaScript feature for lazy-loading modules. This is a core pattern being taught by this skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Dynamic import() is standard ES2020 JavaScript for code splitting - a modern best practice, not a security risk.

low_findings.title: Network Access Detection (FALSE POSITIVE)
low_findings.description: Scanner detected 'Fetch API call' and 'Hardcoded URL' at lines 337, 387, 393, 789, 906-910. This is a FALSE POSITIVE - these are code examples showing how to use the Fetch API and links to legitimate educational resources (MDN, JavaScript.info, You Don't Know JS).
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are code examples in an educational document and links to well-known JavaScript documentation sites.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection (FALSE POSITIVE)
low_findings.description: Scanner detected 'system reconnaissance' at 6 locations. This is a FALSE POSITIVE - the scanner misinterprets references to async iteration and system-related programming patterns as reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are standard JavaScript async patterns and iteration examples, not system reconnaissance.

content.value_statement: Write cleaner, more maintainable JavaScript using ES6+ features like async/await, destructuring, spread operators, and functional programming patterns.
actual_capabilities: Refactor legacy JavaScript to modern ES6+ syntax
Implement async/await patterns for asynchronous operations
Use destructuring and spread operators for cleaner data handling
Apply functional programming patterns like map, filter, reduce
Create lazy-loaded modules with dynamic imports
Build iterators and generators for custom iteration
limitations: Does not cover TypeScript or JavaScript frameworks
Does not include backend Node.js specific patterns
Does not provide runtime execution or code testing
Focused on syntax patterns, not architecture or design patterns

use_cases.title: Legacy Code Modernization
use_cases.description: Refactor older callback-based JavaScript code to use Promises and async/await for better readability and error handling.

use_cases.title: Learning Modern JavaScript
use_cases.description: Understand and apply ES6+ features like destructuring, spread operators, and modules to write more expressive code.

use_cases.title: Code Review Improvements
use_cases.description: Apply modern JavaScript patterns during code reviews to suggest cleaner, more maintainable implementations.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Callback to Async/Await
prompt_templates.prompt:
Convert this callback-based JavaScript function to use async/await syntax. Show the before and after code.

[Insert callback code here]

prompt_templates.title: Apply Destructuring
prompt_templates.prompt:
Refactor this JavaScript code to use object and array destructuring. Make the code more concise and readable.

[Insert code with nested properties or arrays here]
prompt_templates.scenario: Cleaning up code with deep property access

prompt_templates.title: Implement Functional Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt:
Transform this imperative JavaScript code to use functional programming with map, filter, and reduce. Show a chainable pipeline approach.

[Insert loop-based transformation code here]

prompt_templates.title: Lazy Load Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to implement lazy loading with dynamic import() for this feature module. Include error handling and conditional loading logic.
prompt_templates.scenario: Optimizing bundle size with code splitting

best_practices: Use const by default, let only when reassignment is needed
Prefer arrow functions for callbacks to avoid this binding issues
Use async/await over Promise chains for better readability
anti_patterns: Using var instead of const/let - lacks block scoping
Callback hell with nested anonymous functions
Mutating objects directly instead of using immutable patterns

faq.question: What is the difference between let and const?
faq.answer: Use const by default for values that will not be reassigned. Use let only when you need to reassign a variable. Avoid var in modern JavaScript.

faq.question: When should I use async/await over Promises?
faq.answer: Use async/await for cleaner, more readable asynchronous code. It works well for sequential async operations. Use Promise.all for parallel operations.

faq.question: What is destructuring in JavaScript?
faq.answer: Destructuring allows you to extract values from arrays or properties from objects into distinct variables using a concise syntax.

faq.question: What are the benefits of arrow functions?
faq.answer: Arrow functions have shorter syntax, lexical this binding (inherits this from surrounding scope), and cannot be used as constructors.

faq.question: What is the spread operator used for?
faq.answer: The spread operator (...) expands iterables (arrays, strings) into individual elements. It is used for array concatenation, object cloning, and function arguments.

faq.question: How do dynamic imports help performance?
faq.answer: Dynamic import() loads modules on-demand, enabling code splitting. This reduces initial bundle size and improves page load time.
